point_001: The paper is difficult to follow due to its broad scope; it would benefit from a clearer focus and better organization, as too many disparate topics are covered without sufficient depth.
point_002: Key concepts and methods (e.g., the information-theoretic measures used, the 'double descent' phenomenon) are inadequately explained, making it hard for readers to understand their relevance and application in this work.
point_003: The writing quality is problematic: the language is overly complex and convoluted in several sections (abstract, introduction, contributions list), and some portions of text are unnecessarily repeated.
point_004: The figures and diagrams are difficult to read and interpret.
point_005: The Chinese character-level experiments are methodologically questionable: Wubi encoding is designed for fast typing and may not correlate with word meaning, while Pinyin is highly ambiguous (multiple words per syllable) and tones are not accounted for.
point_006: Filtering all languages to a uniform maximum of 300 characters per line creates an unfair comparison, since information density varies greatly across languages (e.g., 300 Chinese characters convey far more information than 300 English characters).
point_007: The claim of 'script bias' in character-level models is overstated, as the experimental evidence is largely limited to Chinese (ZH), which is an expected outlier given its non-alphabetic writing system.
point_008: The use of 'Conditional Language Modeling' (CLM) framing—conditioning on a source language—is potentially misleading and conflates standard language modeling with a machine translation setup; experiments using standard language models (e.g., BERT-style) would be clearer.
point_009: The claim that 'erraticity' is resolved by byte-level representations is not fully supported, as erratic behavior appears to persist at larger data sizes in Figure 3(b), which are more practically relevant.
point_010: The conclusion that linguistic typological information is unnecessary given 'statistical properties of sequence length and vocabulary' is logically circular, since those statistical properties are themselves products of linguistic typology.
point_011: Recommendations for using character-level or byte-level models are not substantiated with concrete results on downstream tasks (e.g., translation) or adequate references to prior work on this topic.
point_012: The paper mentions 'generalizations' at the end of Section 2 but does not appear to follow up with any actual discussion of them.
point_013: Several relevant prior works are missing from the references, including Vania & Lopez (ACL 2017) on morphology and character-to-word representations, Plank et al. (ACL 2016) on multilingual POS tagging, and Ponti et al. (EMNLP 2019) on zero-shot language modeling, which is highly relevant to this paper's conclusions.
